title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
In Pharo, you can check if an element already exists in a set by using the `includes:` message. This message returns true if the receiver (the set in this case) includes the argument as one of its elements, and false otherwise.

Here's how you can modify your code to check if a number already exists in the set before adding it:

```smalltalk
Myfunction
| list length data |
z := Set new.
x := 8.
data := received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do: [ :k | list := data at: k.
    (list < x) and: [ z includes: list not ]
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
    ifTrue: [ z add: list ] ]
```

In this modified version, the code first checks if the list is less than `x`, and then checks if the list is not already in the set `z` using the `includes:` message. If both conditions are true, then the list is added to the set `z`.

Using the `includes:` message ensures that the set does not contain duplicate elements, as it only adds an element if it is not already present in the set. This is more efficient than checking for duplicates after adding all the elements to the set, as it avoids the need for additional checks and potential rejections.

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any further questions.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, there isn't a `switch` statement like in some other programming languages. However, you can achieve similar behavior using `ifThenElse` or `caseOf` (which is a shorthand for multiple `ifThenElse` statements).

In your code, you are trying to implement a simple switch-like behavior to handle different commands. You can simplify your code and make it more readable by using `caseOf`:

```smalltalk
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        | i bo val valInt |
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        bo := i at: 1.
        val := i at: 2.
        valInt := val asInteger.
        (bo = 'down'
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [ bo = 'left'
                ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                ifFalse: [ bo = 'right'
                    ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                    ifFalse: [ bo = 'up'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ]) ].
```

Alternatively, you can use `caseOf`:

```smalltalk
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        | i bo val valInt |
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        bo := i at: 1.
        val := i at: 2.
        valInt := val asInteger.
        bo caseOf: {
            ['down'] -> [turtle down: valInt].
            ['left'] -> [turtle left: valInt].
            ['right'] -> [turtle right: valInt].
            ['up'] -> [turtle up: valInt].
            [else] -> [self assert: false]
        }] ].
```

Both versions do the same thing, but the second one is more concise and easier to read.

Regarding the debugger, you can open it by adding a breakpoint in your code. To add a breakpoint, click on the line number in the Pharo code editor where you want to pause the execution. A small arrow will appear, indicating a breakpoint. Now, when you run your code, the debugger will open when it reaches the breakpoint. You can then step through the code, inspect variables, and understand what's happening.

If your code is not throwing an exception, it's hard to provide a solution for opening the debugger without one. However, you can open the debugger manually by executing `World openDebuggee` in a Workspace or a Playground. Then, you can use the debugger as needed.

Here's an example of how your code could look using a more object-oriented approach with a dictionary to map commands to methods:

```smalltalk
commandMap := Dictionary new.
commandMap at: 'down' put: [ :anInteger | turtle down: anInteger ].
commandMap at: 'left' put: [ :anInteger | turtle left: anInteger ].
commandMap at: 'right' put: [ :anInteger | turtle right: anInteger ].
commandMap at: 'up' put: [ :anInteger | turtle up: anInteger ].

endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        | i bo val valInt |
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        bo := i at: 1.
        val := i at: 2.
        valInt := val asInteger.
        (commandMap at: bo ifAbsent: [ self assert: false ]) value: valInt
    ]
].
```

This version separates command handling from the command parsing and execution, making it more modular and easier to maintain.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?

```smalltalk
| result |
result := Shell sh: 'ls -l'.
Transcript show: result; cr.
```

In this example, the `sh` method runs the `ls -l` command, and the output is stored in the `result` variable. The `Transcript` is then used to display the result in the environment's Transcript window.

If you need to capture the output of the command, you can use the `#pipe` method instead, which returns a `Process` object. This object represents the subprocess running the shell command and allows you to interact with its input/output/error streams. Here's an example:

```smalltalk

errorStream := Shell stderr pipe.
output := Shell stdout pipe.

Shell sh: 'ls -l' stdin: commandStream stdout: output stderr: errorStream.

Transcript show: 'Output: ', result; cr.

result := errorStream upToEnd.

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
In Smalltalk, you can create a new `Date` or `DateTime` object with specific day, month, and year values using the `#year:month:day:` method provided by these classes.

Here's how you can create a new `Date` object:

```smalltalk
| today |
today := Date today.
newDate := Date
	year: today year
	month: 5
	day: 17.
```

In this example, we create a new `Date` object called `newDate` representing May 17th of the current year.

Similarly, you can create a new `DateTime` object:

```smalltalk
| today |
today := DateTime now.
newDateTime := DateTime
	year: today year
	month: 5
	day: 17
	hour: 13
	minute: 45
	second: 0.
```

This creates a new `DateTime` object called `newDateTime` representing May 17th, 2022, at 1:45 PM (with seconds set to 0).

The `#asYear` method you mentioned is not relevant in this context, as it is used to convert an integer to a `Date` object representing the beginning of that year. It does not allow setting day, month, and year values.

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} .

```smalltalk
aDictionary := Dictionary new.
aDictionary at: 'Rankable1' put: 8.5.

```

Now, you can create an OrderedCollection of the keys sorted by their corresponding values in descending order as follows:

```smalltalk

```smalltalk

    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
Yes, there is a simpler way to check if two arrays contain the same elements, regardless of their order. You can use the `sortedBlock` and `equals` methods provided by the `Array` class in Smalltalk. Here's a more concise and readable solution based on your example:

```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).

(arr1 sortedBlock: [:a :b | a <= b]) = (arr2 sortedBlock: [:a :b | a <= b])
    ifTrue: [^ Transcript show: 'Arrays contain the same elements'; cr]
    ifFalse: [^ Transcript show: 'Arrays do not contain the same elements'; cr].
```

In this example, we sort both arrays using the same block `[:a :b | a <= b]`. If the sorted arrays are equal, then the original arrays contain the same elements. If they are not equal, then the original arrays do not contain the same elements.

Here's a breakdown of the example:

1. Define arrays `arr1` and `arr2`.
2. Compare the sorted arrays using the `=` operator.
3. If they are equal, print "Arrays contain the same elements".
4. If they are not equal, print "Arrays do not contain the same elements".

This solution works for arrays of any type, as long as the elements implement the `#=` method for equality checks.

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation:
Both Squeak and Pharo are popular choices for beginners in Smalltalk, but Pharo is generally preferred due to its emphasis on innovation, active community, and open-source nature. Here's a comparison of the two:

Squeak:
- Squeak is the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework (Pharo also supports Seaside).
- Squeak's core contains code that has been contributed under the MIT license.
- Squeak started as a modern implementation of Smalltalk-80 and is the basis for Pharo.
- Squeak can run on various platforms, including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi.

Pharo:
- Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed language inspired by Smalltalk, with a simple syntax that can fit on a postcard.
- It offers a live coding environment that allows programmers to modify code while it executes.
- Pharo has a powerful IDE, a rich library, and a culture that encourages changes and improvements.
- Pharo is the vehicle for various innovative projects, from multimedia applications to commercial web development environments.
- Pharo does not just copy the past; it reinvents the essence of Smalltalk with evolutionary and incremental changes.
- Pharo is highly portable and can run on the same platforms as Squeak.

As of now, Pharo is the more popular choice for beginners due to its active community, incremental improvements, and modern features. Pharo's development team includes experts in the field, ensuring it remains a reliable and innovative platform for professional software development and research into dynamic languages and environments.

When choosing between Squeak and Pharo, consider the following factors:

- The community: Pharo has a more active community, which means more resources, tutorials, and support for beginners.
- The project's focus: Squeak is great if you want a more stable, traditional Smalltalk environment, while Pharo is better if you're interested in cutting-edge features and language improvements.
- Compatibility: Pharo is compatible with Squeak's plugins and extensions, but the reverse is not always true. Pharo is designed to be more modular and easier to maintain than Squeak.

For a beginner, Pharo is recommended due to its active community and modern features. Most of the introductory material in this book will work with any Pharo version, so you can start with Pharo 5.0 and upgrade as needed. Pharo's website provides easy-to-follow installation instructions for various platforms.

Sources:
- Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, Stéphane Ducasse, Damien Pollet, Andrew P. Black, Oscar Nierstrasz, Damien Cassou, and Marcus Denker, Leanpub, 2016, pp. 1-2, 7.
- Pharo By Example, 1st Edition, Andrew P. Black, Stéphane Ducasse, Oscar Nierstrasz, Damien Pollet, Damien Cassou, and Marcus Denker, Leanpub, 2011, pp. 1-2, 7.

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
Yes, it is possible to add a string to a String instance via a method in Smalltalk, specifically in Pharo. You can define a method similar to the example you provided, where you send the `append:` message to the String instance with the text you want to add. Here's an example:

```smalltalk
renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'
```

In Smalltalk, strings are mutable collections, which means that you can modify their contents using the `at:put:` message. Additionally, you can use the `replaceAll:with:` or `replaceFrom:to:with:` messages to modify existing strings.

When concatenating strings, it's more efficient to use `nextPut:` and `nextPutAll:` on a `WriteStream` object instead of the comma concatenation operator `,`.

Here's an example of how to use `WriteStream` to concatenate strings:

```smalltalk
| tempStream |
tempStream := WriteStream on: String new.
(1 to: 100000) do: [ :i | tempStream nextPutAll: i asString; space ].
result := tempStream contents.
```

In this example, we create a new `WriteStream` object and use it to concatenate the strings returned by `i asString` and `' '`. The `contents` message returns the resulting string.

You can also modify an existing string using the `replaceAll:with:` or `replaceFrom:to:with:` messages. Here's an example:

```smalltalk
s := 'no hurries'.
s replaceAll: $n with: $N.
s
>>> 'No hurries'
```

In this example, we replace all occurrences of `$n` with `$N` in the string `'no hurries'`.

When adding methods to a class, you can use the Pharo browser to create a new method template and replace it with your code. Don't forget to compile the method using the `CMD-s` key combination.

Here's an example of how to add a method to the `String` class:

1. Open the Pharo browser and select the `String` class.
2. Select the protocol where you want to add the method (in this case, the `no messages` protocol).
3. Create a new method template by selecting the template text in the editing pane and replacing it with your code.
4. Compile the method by pressing `CMD-s`.

Here's an example of a method that returns the upper-case version of a string:

```smalltalk
uppercaseVersion
	"Return an upper-case version of the receiver"
	^ self asUppercase
```

In this example, the method `uppercaseVersion` returns the upper-case version of the receiver string by sending the `asUppercase` message to the receiver.

Overall, Pharo provides a variety of ways to add strings to a `String` instance via a method. You can use the `append:` message to add text to the end of the string, or use the `at:put:` message to modify its contents. Additionally, you can use the `replaceAll:with:` or `replaceFrom:to:with:` messages to modify existing strings, and use `WriteStream` to concatenate strings more efficiently.

title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)
explanation:
In Smalltalk, you can check if an object is an instance of a given class or a subclass of it using the method `isKindOf:` which is defined in the `Object` class. This method answers whether the receiver’s class is either the same as, or a subclass of the argument class.

For example, if you have an object `1`, you can check if it is an instance of `SmallInteger`, `Integer`, `Number`, or `Object` as follows:

```
1 isKindOf: SmallInteger
>>> true
1 isKindOf: Integer
>>> true
1 isKindOf: Number
>>> true
1 isKindOf: Object
>>> true
1 isKindOf: String
>>> false
```

In this example, `1` is an instance of `SmallInteger`, which is a subclass of `Integer`, which is a subclass of `Number`, which is a subclass of `Object`. Therefore, `1` is an instance of all these classes. However, `1` is not an instance of `String`, so the expression `1 isKindOf: String` returns `false`.

You can also use the `isMemberOf:` method to check if an object is an instance of a specific class. However, this method requires that the receiver must be precisely the argument class, and not one of its subclasses.

For example, if you have an object `1.5`, you can check if it is an instance of `Float`, `Integer`, or `Number` as follows:

```
1.5 isMemberOf: Float
>>> true
1.5 isMemberOf: Integer
>>> false
1.5 isMemberOf: Number
>>> true
```

In this example, `1.5` is an instance of `Float`, which is a subclass of `Number`. Therefore, `1.5 isMemberOf: Number` returns `true`. However, `1.5` is not an instance of `Integer`, so `1.5 isMemberOf: Integer` returns `false`.

Note that Pharo has some strange equality behavior compared to other Smalltalks. For example, a symbol and a string can be equal, which may cause unexpected results when checking class membership.

References:

* Page 183: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
* Page 184: isKindOf: method
* Page 301: isMemberOf: method
* Page 348-349: Class membership rules.

title: How to print an integer to transcript
body:
I've tried the code

|myNum|
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).

, but Pharo crashes upon running this code.
stackoverflow_answer:
You can't create a SmallInteger using new:. You already have the number you want to print. It's already a SmallInteger. Just use it like such:

Transcript show: 0 printString
explanation:
The issue with the provided code is that you're trying to print the string representation of an uninitialized `SmallInteger` object. Instead, you should assign a specific integer value to `myNum`.

To print an integer to the transcript, you can follow these steps:

1. Initialize or assign a value to your integer variable.
2. Use the `Transcript show:` method to print the integer.

Here's an example:

```smalltalk
| myNum |
myNum := 42.
Transcript show: myNum; cr. "The 'cr' message adds a newline after the integer."
```

Pharo's Transcript is a system console used for logging system messages, and you can display a string in the Transcript by selecting the text and using the keyboard shortcut (CMD-p) or by using the 'Do it' menu item. This will compile the expression, execute it, send the `printString` message to the result, and display the resulting string.

Additionally, if you want to see the result of an expression without printing it, you can use the 'Do it' keyboard shortcut (ALT-d) or the 'Do it' menu item. However, this will not display the result if the playground doesn't know what to do with the answer, as in the case of your original code.

Relevant sources:

* Pages 16, 18, and 63-64 of "Pharo By Example"
* Section "2.8 Playgrounds and Transcripts"
* Figure 2-12, "Executing an expression: displaying a string in the Transcript"
* Figure 2-13, "Inspecting a simple number using Inspect"

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
Yes, it is possible to match all words in a phrase using regular expressions. The issue with your current regex is that it only matches words that are followed by a non-word character. In the phrase "hello world", it matches "hello" but not "world" because "world" is at the end of the line.

To match all words in a phrase, you can use the word boundary assertion \b at both the beginning and end of the regex. This will match words that are surrounded by non-word characters, such as whitespace or punctuation. Here's an example:
```ruby
'hello world' matchesRegex: '\b\w*\b'  # Answer: true
```
In this example, \b matches the empty string at the beginning and end of a word, and \w* matches zero or more word characters (letters, digits, or underscores). The result is a regex that matches all words in a phrase.

If you want to extract all the words in a phrase, you can use the `allRegexMatches:` message, which returns a collection of all matches (substrings of the receiver string) of the regular expression. Here's an example:
```python
| text words |
text := 'hello world'.
words := text allRegexMatches: '\b\w*\b'.
words  "Answer: #('hello' 'world')"
```
In this example, `allRegexMatches:` evaluates a one-argument block for every match of the regular expression within the receiver string and collects the results as a sequenceable collection.

For more information and examples, you can refer to the Pharo By Example textbook, pages 339-344.

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, both `self` and `yourself` are pseudo-variables used within a method body. The key difference between them lies in the object they represent when used in a message send.

`self` represents the receiver of the message, and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver (Pg. 103, 105). This means that when you send a message with `self`, you are using the current object to call the method. For example, in the following code:

```smalltalk
anObject someMethod
```

`someMethod` will be executed in the context of `anObject`.

On the other hand, `yourself` is similar to `self`, but with a crucial difference: when used in a method, it always answers the receiver of the current message. It is similar to writing `^ self` at the end of a method, but with a subtle distinction. The Pharo object model treats instances as class instances, created using `yourself` instead of a specific class name (Pg. 95). For example, in the following code:

```smalltalk
WebServer uniqueInstance
```

Using `yourself` ensures that the correct subclass instance is created, depending on the context.

In summary, `self` is used to represent the receiver of the current message, and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver, while `yourself` is similar to `self` but always answers the receiver of the current message, ensuring the correct object instance is used in various contexts.

supported_facts_labels: ['` self ` represents the receiver of the message , and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver ( Pg . 103 , 105 ) .']

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, you can initialize instance variables in several ways, but the most common way is to do it in the class side's initialize method, using lazy initialization or not, depending on the use case.

Lazy initialization is useful when users don't expect the class to be initialized immediately or when initialization is expensive. For example, you can use lazy initialization to open a database connection or a network socket only when needed. To implement lazy initialization, you can use an ifNil: check in the accessor method to initialize the instance variable only if it's nil.

On the other hand, if you want to initialize an instance variable with a specific value every time an object is created, you can do it in the class side's initialize method without using lazy initialization. For example, you can define a class variable and initialize it in the class side's initialize method. Then, you can define a class method to access the class variable and use it to initialize the instance variable.

Here's an example of how to initialize a Color object with a specific color name, either using a class method or a setter method:
```smalltalk
| aColor |
aColor := Color named: 'red'. "using a class method"

aColor := Color new.
aColor name: 'red'. "using a setter method"
```
Using a class method is more concise and readable, but using a setter method gives you more flexibility and control over the initialization process. It's up to you to choose the best approach depending on your needs and preferences.

In summary, in Pharo Smalltalk, you can initialize instance variables using lazy initialization or not, depending on whether you want to delay the initialization or not. You can define a class method or a setter method to initialize the instance variable with a specific value. The choice depends on your use case and personal preference.

References:

* Pharo By Example, 2nd edition, pages 6.6, 6.9, 108, 109, 111.
* Listing 6-29, Initializing the Color class.
* Figure 6-27, Instance and class methods accessing different variables.

title: For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->

This is my code:

GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

What can I do?
stackoverflow_answer:
Some interesting things to consider:

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter

It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.

2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'

For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)

3. The Array named lista has 31 elements, not 30

The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.

4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error

The code

  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).

There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

1 to: 10 do: [:i | self somethingWith: i]     "<- missing dot here"
self somethingElse

the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.

5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been

#(0 1) atRandom

6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with "toy" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
explanation:
The issue with your code is that you're using the wrong method for filling the array with random numbers. You should use `atPut:` instead of `at: put:` to correctly place the random numbers into the array. The `at: put:` method is used to replace an existing element at a given index, while `atPut:` is used to put a new element at a specific index.

Here's the corrected version of your code:

```smalltalk
GenList
    | lista |
     lista := Array new: 31.
     1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista atPut: (2 atRandom - 1) ].
    ^lista
```

In this corrected version, `2 atRandom - 1` generates a random number that is either 0 or 1, and then it is placed at the current index using `atPut:`.

For loops in Pharo Smalltalk use the `to:do:` message sent to a Number, which acts as the initial value of a loop counter. The two arguments are the upper bound, and a block that takes the current value of the loop counter as its argument.

In Pharo, arrays are indexed starting from 1, and the main protocol to access array elements is the method `at:` and `at:put:`. However, when filling an array with values, you should use `atPut:` instead of `at: put:`.

For more information, refer to page 67, 203, and 213-214 of the "Pharo By Example" textbook.
supporting_facts_labels: ['The two arguments are the upper bound , and a block that takes the current value of the loop counter as its argument : result : = String new .', 'The main protocol to access array elements is the method at : and at : put : .']
supported_facts_labels: ['The two arguments are the upper bound , and a block that takes the current value of the loop counter as its argument .', 'In Pharo , arrays are indexed starting from 1 , and the main protocol to access array elements is the method ` at : ` and ` at : put : ` .', 'However , when filling an array with values , you should use ` atPut : ` instead of ` at : put : ` .']

title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, you can access class variables by providing class methods to explicitly get or set their values. This is because, in Pharo, there is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object. Instead, Pharo uses a message-passing mechanism, where you send messages to objects to invoke methods that manipulate their state.

In your case, to assign the year 2012 to the `start-time` instance of `Class1`, you need to define a class method in `Class1` to set the `year` variable. First, let's make `year`, `month`, `day`, `hour`, and `minute` be class variables in `Class1`.

```smalltalk
Object subclass: #Class1
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: 'year month day hour minute'
    poolDictionaries: ''
    category: 'YourCategory'.
```

Now, add a class method to set the `year` class variable:

```smalltalk
Class1 classsideDefinition: #setYear: value:
    "Set the year for the receiver (Class1 class)"
    year := value.
```

Now, you can assign the year 2012 to the `start-time` instance of `Class1` like this:

```smalltalk
startTime := Class1 new.
startTime class setYear: 2012.
```

Keep in mind that class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself. This means that changing the `year` value using one instance will affect the value for all other instances.

References:
- Page 109: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
- Page 89: Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself
- Page 92: Class instance variables

(Note: I made assumptions regarding your desired behavior and the actual code structure since the provided text chunks didn't include the full context or exact code.)

supported_facts_labels: ['This is because , in Pharo , there is no language syntax that provides direct access to the instance variables of any other object .', 'Instead , Pharo uses a message - passing mechanism , where you send messages to objects to invoke methods that manipulate their state .', '` ` `    Keep in mind that class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself .', 'References :   - Page 109 : How to access class variables in Smalltalk   - Page 89 : Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself   - Page 92 :']

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation:
The error in your method declaration is due to the incorrect syntax of keyword messages. In Pharo, keyword messages consist of a message keyword, followed by a colon, then the argument value. Your current method declaration is missing the colons after each keyword. The correct syntax for your method declaration would be:

MethodName: arg1: argValue1 arg2: argValue2
^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

You would then call this method like this:

ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

In Pharo, arrays are 1-based, and you can access and modify their elements using the `at:`, `at:put:`, and `atAll:` methods. Here's an example of creating an array, putting values in it, and accessing its first element:

| anArray |
anArray := Array new: 5.
anArray at: 1 put: 4.
anArray at: 2 put: 3.
anArray first

Pharo supports three kinds of messages: unary messages (no argument), binary messages (one argument), and keyword messages (arbitrary number of arguments). Here's an example of each type:

1 factorial
1 + 2
2 raisedTo: 6

Lastly, Pharo methods can have multiple arguments. When calling such a method, you need to provide all the required arguments in the correct order. Here's an example of a method with two arguments, and calling it:

exampleMethod: arg1 arg2: arg2
some code here

exampleMethod: 1 arg2: 2

Remember that in Pharo, you send messages to objects, and it's up to the receiver to decide how to respond. This is different from some other programming languages where you invoke methods on objects.
supporting_facts_labels: ['Keyword messages are messages consisting of one or more keywords , each ending with a colon ( :)']
supported_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , keyword messages consist of a message keyword , followed by a colon']

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.
explanation:
In Pharo, you can't create a constructor method like `newName:newAge:` as you did in your example. Instead, you should use the `new` method, which is already defined in the `Object` class and is used to create new instances of a class. If you want to perform some actions when a new instance is created, you can override the `new` method in your class. However, it's important to note that the `new` method should always call `super new` to ensure that the superclass's `new` method is executed.

In your case, you can define the `name` and `age` instance variables in the class definition, like this:
```ruby
Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'
```
Then, you can create a new instance of the `Person` class and initialize its instance variables using the `new` method, like this:
```arduino
| objPerson |
objPerson := Person new.
objPerson name: 'Carl'.
objPerson age: 10.
```
Alternatively, you can override the `new` method in the `Person` class to initialize the instance variables with the given values, like this:
```smalltalk
Person >> new
    "Create a new instance of the Person class and initialize its instance variables with the given name and age."

    ^ super new
        name: 'Carl';
        age: 10;
        yourself
```
Then, you can create a new instance of the `Person` class using the `new` method, like this:
```arduino
| objPerson |
objPerson := Person new.
```
This will create a new instance of the `Person` class and initialize its instance variables with the default values specified in the `new` method (in this case, 'Carl' for the `name` instance variable and 10 for the `age` instance variable).

Here's the complete code example:
```ruby
Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

Person >> new
    "Create a new instance of the Person class and initialize its instance variables with the given name and age."

    ^ super new
        name: 'Carl';
        age: 10;
        yourself

Person >> name: aName age: anAge
    "Set the value of the name and age instance variables."

    name := aName.
    age := anAge.

| objPerson |
objPerson := Person new.
Transcript show: objPerson name; cr.
Transcript show: objPerson age; cr.
```
This code defines the `Person` class with two instance variables (`name` and `age`), overrides the `new` method to initialize the instance variables with the default values 'Carl' and 10, and defines a `name:age:` method to set the values of the instance variables. The example code then creates a new instance of the `Person` class using the `new` method, and displays the values of the `name` and `age` instance variables in the Transcript.

Sources:

* Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, page 6.6: "Every class has a superclass"
* Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, page 104: "Method lookup follows the inheritance chain"
* Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, page 119: "Some of the key tools of the Pharo environment"
* Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, page 3.4: "Adding methods to a class"
* Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, page 37: "A first application"
* Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, page 93: "The Pharo object model"
* Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, page 97: "Abstract methods and abstract classes"
supporting_facts_labels: ['* Method lookup follows the inheritance chain ; self sends are dynamic and start the method lookup in the class of the receiver , whereas su113 The Pharo object model per sends start the method lookup in the superclass of class in which the super send is written .', '6.6 Every class has a superclass Each class in Pharo inherits its behaviour and the description of its structure from a single superclass .']

title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
The error message you're encountering, "MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript," is due to the fact that you're trying to use the Transcript as the receiver of the message, which is not allowed. In Smalltalk, the Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages, and it is a kind of system console. You can use it to print the contents of an OrderedCollection object by sending the appropriate messages to the collection object itself, not to the Transcript.

To print the contents of an OrderedCollection object using the Transcript, you can use the `printOn:` method to generate a string representation of the collection and then print that string using the Transcript. Here's an example:
```
oc := OrderedCollection new.
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).

|stream|
stream := WriteStream on: String new.
oc do: [:each | stream print: each; space].
Transcript show: stream contents; cr.
```
In this example, we first create an OrderedCollection object and add some elements to it. We then create a WriteStream object and use the `do:` method to iterate over the elements of the collection, printing each one to the stream with the `print:` method. Finally, we print the contents of the stream to the Transcript using the `show:` method and the `cr` method to add a carriage return at the end.

The reason why you can't use the Transcript as the receiver of your code is because the Transcript is not a collection, and it doesn't have a method that can display an OrderedCollection object directly. By using the `printOn:` method to generate a string representation of the collection, we can print that string to the Transcript using the `show:` method.

You can find more information about Streaming over collections, WriteStreams, and the Transcript in pages 226-228, 227, and 16 of the Pharo By Example textbook, respectively.
supporting_facts_labels: ['The Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages .', '217 Basic classes The following code yields the expected result : | collection | collection : = OrderedCollection new .']
supported_facts_labels: ['In Smalltalk , the Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages , and it is a kind of system console .', "Here 's an example :   ` ` `   oc : = OrderedCollection new ."]

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
In Pharo, you can write `if elifelseif` using the messages `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, and `ifTrue:ifFalse:`. Pharo does not have a special syntax for control constructs, instead, these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections, with blocks as arguments.

Here is an example of how you can write an `if elifelseif` using the messages `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, and `ifTrue:ifFalse:`:

```
| x |
x := 25.
(x = 25)
ifTrue: [ 'x is equal to 25' ]
ifFalse: [ 
    (x > 25)
    ifTrue: [ 'x is greater than 25' ]
    ifFalse: [ 'x is less than 25' ]
].
```

In this example, the expression `(x = 25)` will return a boolean value, if it's true, it will execute the block `['x is equal to 25']`, if it's false, it will evaluate the next condition `(x > 25)` and so on.

You can also use the message `ifTrue:ifFalse:` to simplify the above example:

```
| x |
x := 25.
(x = 25)
ifTrue: [ 'x is equal to 25' ]
ifFalse: [ (x > 25) ifTrue: [ 'x is greater than 25' ] ifFalse: [ 'x is less than 25' ] ].
```

It's important to notice that Pharo's conditionals and loops are inlined by the compiler and transformed to comparison and jump bytecodes, this allows Pharo to be more efficient than other languages that use virtual machines. However, this also means that attempting to send messages like `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:` or `ifTrue:ifFalse:` to non-boolean receivers will result in an exception from the VM.

You can find more information about this in the Pharo By Example book, specifically in chapter 4.6 "Conditionals and loops in a nutshell" and chapter 10.5 "Booleans"

Regards,

References:

* Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, Stéphane Ducasse, Damien Pollet, Andrew P. Black, Oscar Nierstrasz, Jan Vrany, and Tudor Gîrba, page 66, 83, 84, 195, 196, and 198.
* Pharo By Example, 3rd Edition, Stéphane Ducasse, Damien Pollet, Andrew P. Black, Oscar Nierstrasz, Jan Vrany, and Tudor Gîrba, page 17.
supporting_facts_labels: ['4.6 Conditionals and loops in a nutshell Pharo offers no special syntax for control constructs .', 'For instance , instead of an if - then - else control structure , conditionals are expressed as messages ( such as ifTrue : ) sent to Boolean objects .']
supported_facts_labels: ['Pharo does not have a special syntax for control constructs , instead , these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans , numbers , and collections , with blocks as arguments .', 'However , this also means that attempting to send messages like ` ifTrue : ` , ` ifFalse : ` or ` ifTrue : ifFalse : ` to non - boolean receivers will result in an exception from the VM .']

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
The `subclassResponsibility` method is used to indicate that a subclass should implement a specific method. It is a way of setting up a framework for the behavior of a class' subclasses. When a method sending `subclassResponsibility` is executed, it will raise an error indicating that the subclass should have overridden the method. This is useful for abstract classes, which are classes that exist to be subclassed rather than instantiated.

In your example, you have an inheritance tree with `AbstractDictionary` at the top, and `TreeBasedDictionary`, `AbstractArrayDictionary`, `SimpleDictionary`, and `FastDictionary` as its subclasses. You want to implement the `size` method in `AbstractDictionary` and have it call the `size` method in the appropriate subclass.

You cannot simply call `^self size` in `AbstractDictionary`'s `size` method, because that would lead to an infinite loop. Instead, you should use `subclassResponsibility` to indicate that the subclasses are responsible for implementing their own version of the `size` method. This way, when the `size` method is called on an instance of a subclass, it will look for the method in the subclass first, and if it doesn't find it, it will look in the superclass and execute the `subclassResponsibility` method, raising an error.

You should not remove the method from `AbstractArrayDictionary`, because it is still an abstract method that needs to be overridden by its subclasses. However, you can implement the `size` method in `TreeBasedDictionary` and the other subclasses of `AbstractArrayDictionary` as needed.

It is also worth noting that when a method sending `subclassResponsibility` is executed, it will raise an error indicating which abstract method has been invoked by using `thisContext sender selector printString`. This can be useful for debugging purposes.

References:

* Page 97: Abstract classes and methods, using `subclassResponsibility`
* Page 186-187: Error handling and `subclassResponsibility`
* Page 311: Intelligent breakpoints and `subclassResponsibility`
* Page 113-114: The Pharo object model and method lookup
* Page 187-188: Abstract methods as placeholders and `subclassResponsibility`

Note: The provided text chunks were used to support the answer, but were not included in the answer itself to avoid redundancy and improve readability.
