title: For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->

This is my code:

GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

What can I do?
stackoverflow_answer:
Some interesting things to consider:

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter

It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.

2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'

For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)

3. The Array named lista has 31 elements, not 30

The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.

4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error

The code

  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).

There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

1 to: 10 do: [:i | self somethingWith: i]     "<- missing dot here"
self somethingElse

the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.

5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been

#(0 1) atRandom

6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with "toy" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
explanation:
In Pharo, the most generic ways to create instances of collections are through the use of the messages new: aSize and with: anElement. The message new: anInteger creates a collection of size anInteger whose elements will all be nil. The message with: anObject creates a collection and adds anObject to the created collection. Different collections will realize this behavior differently. For example, an Array with: 1 will create an array with a single element, 1, while a Set with: anObject will create a new set with anObject as its only element.

Additionally, Pharo provides several ways to create collections with initial elements. For example, you can use the methods with:with:, with:with:with:, with:with:with:with:, and withAll: to create collections with initial elements. The method withAll: aCollection can also be used to create a new collection that contains all of the elements of an existing collection.

It is important to note that some collections, such as Arrays, are fixed-size and do not allow elements to be added or removed. Other collections, such as OrderedCollection and Set, are variable-size and allow elements to be added or removed as needed.

To access elements of a collection, Pharo provides the messages at: and at:put:. The message at: anInteger returns the element at index anInteger, while the message at: anInteger put: anObject puts anObject at index anInteger.

To sort a collection, you can use the message asSortedCollection. This message returns a new collection that contains the same elements as the original collection, but sorted in ascending order.

To convert a collection to another kind of collection, you can use the messages asArray, asOrderedCollection, asSet, and asDictionary. These messages return a new collection of the specified type that contains the same elements as the original collection.

Here are some examples of creating and manipulating collections in Pharo:

* Array with: 1 with: 2 with: 3 with: 4 with: 5 with: 6 >>> #(1 2 3 4 5 6)
* OrderedCollection withAll: #(1 2 3 4 5) >>> an OrderedCollection(1 2 3 4 5)
* SortedCollection withAll: #(7 3 1 3) >>> a SortedCollection(1 3 3 7)
* Set withAll: #(7 1 2 3 4 5) >>> a Set(7 1 2 3 4 5)
* Dictionary newFrom: { 'a' -> 1 . 'b' -> 2 . 'c' -> 3 } >>> a Dictionary('a'->1 'b'->2 'c'->3)
* (1 to: 5) asOrderedCollection addAll: '678'; yourself >>> an OrderedCollection(1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8)
* 'hello' asSortedCollection as: String >>> 'ehllo'
* String newFrom: 'hello' asSortedCollection >>> 'ehllo'
* String withAll: 'hello' asSortedCollection >>> 'ehllo'
* { 5 . 2/ -3 . 5.21 } asSortedCollection >>> a SortedCollection((-2/3) 5 5.21)

I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.

title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, to access a class variable, you need to provide class methods to access them. Class variables are shared amongst all the instances of a class as well as the class itself. Their lifetime is the same as that of the class.

In your case, you have two classes: Class1 and Class2. Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute, while Class2 has start-time and end-time. The start-time and end-time are initialized as new instances of Class1. To assign the year 2012 to the start-time, you need to access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time.

To achieve this, you first need to define a class variable in Class1 to store the year. Then, create a class method to access and modify this class variable. Here's how you can do it:

1. In Class1, define the class variable 'Year' and a class method to access it:

```smalltalk
Class1 classVariableNames: 'Year'.
Class1 instanceVariableNames: 'year month day hour minute'.
Class1 classVariableNames instVarNamed: 'Year' put: 1900. "set initial year to 1900"

Class1 >>year
    ^ Class1 Year

Class1 >>year: aYear
    Class1 Year: aYear
```

2. Now, in Class2, you can access and set the 'year' variable for start-time and end-time:

```smalltalk
Class2 >>initialize
    start-time := Class1 new.
    end-time := Class1 new.
    start-time year: 2012. "set year for start-time"
    end-time year: 2013. "set year for end-time"
```

Remember, to adhere to best practices, it's recommended to limit the use of global variables. Instead, use class instance variables or class variables and provide class methods to access them. This approach promotes encapsulation and makes your code more maintainable.

References:
- Page 109, Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition
- Pages 89, 92, 111, Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition (related to concepts of encapsulation and global variables)

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation: In Pharo, the syntax is minimal and is based on sending messages to objects. An expression in Pharo is built up from a small number of primitive elements, including message sends, assignments, closures, and returns. The syntax is designed to be read aloud as a kind of pidgin English, with message sends consisting of a receiver, a method selector, and a set of arguments. There are three types of messages: unary, binary, and keyword messages, which are sent in that order. Variables are declared using vertical bars, and assignments are made using the := operator. Blocks, which are lexical closures, are defined using square brackets and are executed by sending the value message to them. The debugger provides a high-level GUI to inspect and modify the state of a running program, and code can be shared using Monticello to manage an external repository.

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.
explanation: In Pharo, a global variable can be defined by performing Do it on an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier, or by executing Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil. To remove a global variable, execute Smalltalk globals removeKey: #AGlobalName. Global variables are accessible everywhere and are stored in a namespace called Smalltalk, which is implemented as an instance of the class SystemDictionary. Globals include Smalltalk, World, and ActiveHand. Class variables are shared between instances and classes, and are stored in the class side of a class. They are defined as class variables of dedicated classes and are named with a capital letter. Global and class variables should be avoided if possible, and class instance variables or global methods should be used instead. Pool variables are variables that are shared between several classes that may not be related by inheritance. They were originally stored in pool dictionaries but should now be defined as class variables of dedicated classes.

title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
In Smalltalk, the Transcript is an object used for logging system messages, and it is not the receiver of your code because it does not respond to the message 'Transcript show:cr.' The correct way to print the contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript is to use the 'printOn:' method, which sends the textual representation of the object to the Transcript.

Here's an example:

```smalltalk
oc := OrderedCollection new.
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: oc printString; cr.
```

In this example, the 'printString' message is sent to the 'oc' object, which returns a string representation of the OrderedCollection. This string is then sent to the Transcript using the 'show:' message.

The reason why your original code does not work is that 'Transcript' is not a collection, and therefore, it does not understand the 'add:' message. The 'self' keyword in your original code refers to the current object, which is not a collection, and 'oc' is also not a collection but an instance of OrderedCollection. Therefore, neither 'self' nor 'oc' can be the receiver of the 'Transcript' message.

Furthermore, the 'Transcript show: self; cr.' message sends the textual representation of the current object to the Transcript, but 'self' is not a collection, so it does not make sense to send the 'Transcript' message to 'self.'

In summary, the Transcript is not the receiver of your code because it does not respond to the message 'Transcript show:cr.' To print the contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript, you can use the 'printOn:' method to send the textual representation of the object to the Transcript.

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
In Pharo, control constructs such as if-elseif-elseif are expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections, with blocks as arguments. Specifically, conditionals are expressed using the messages ifTrue:, ifFalse:, or ifTrue:ifFalse:. For example, to check three conditions, you can write:

```smalltalk
(x > 25)
	ifTrue: [ DoSomething ]
	ifFalse: [
		(x = 25)
			ifTrue: [ DoSomethingElse ]
			ifFalse: [ DoSomethingDifferent ] ]
```

In this example, if x is greater than 25, then the message ifTrue: is sent to the boolean result of (x > 25) with the block [ DoSomething ] as an argument, and the specified code is executed. If x is not greater than 25, then the message ifFalse: is sent, and the boolean expression (x = 25) is evaluated. If the result is true, then the block [ DoSomethingElse ] is executed. If the result is false, then the block [ DoSomethingDifferent ] is executed.

This approach to control flow is based on the principle of sending messages to objects, which is a fundamental aspect of Pharo's syntax. Expressions are composed of a small number of primitive elements, including message sends, assignments, closures, and returns. There are only six keywords in Pharo, and there is no syntax for control structures or declaring new classes. Instead, nearly everything is achieved by sending messages to objects.

One advantage of this approach is that it enables a collection-based high-order programming style, which is a central tenet of Pharo. This style enables the creation of new collections containing precisely those elements that meet some criterion, using messages such as select: or reject:. The blocks used in these messages can contain temporary variables, and they are not evaluated until you send the block a value message with the correct number of arguments.

Overall, Pharo's approach to control flow is based on sending messages to objects, which enables a collection-based high-order programming style and promotes good code design. While it may take some time to get used to this syntax, it offers a powerful and flexible way to express complex control flow in Pharo.

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
The `subclassResponsibility` method is used in abstract classes in Pharo to signal that a subclass should have implemented a particular method. When the abstract method is called, it raises an error indicating that the subclass has not implemented the method. This is useful for ensuring that subclasses provide their own implementation of certain methods required by the abstract class.

In your example, you have an `AbstractDictionary` class with several subclasses, such as `TreeBasedDictionary` and `AbstractArrayDictionary`. These subclasses all implement the `at:put:` and `at:` methods, and you want to pull up the `at:put:` method to the `AbstractDictionary` class. However, you also want to allow for the possibility of duplicate keys, so you implement a new method `at:put:duplicate:` in the `TreeBasedDictionary` and `AbstractArrayDictionary` classes. When the `at:put:` method is called on an instance of one of these subclasses, it will not find the method in the instance, but will find it in the superclass (`AbstractDictionary`). This will then call `self` with `at:put:duplicate:`, which is implemented by the three aforementioned classes. The `self` in this case refers to the instance of the class that made the initial call, and the `at:put:duplicate:` method is sent to this instance.

In the `AbstractArrayDictionary` and its subclasses, you have a `size` method that is implemented as follows:
```ruby
size
    "dictionary size"
    self subclassResponsibility
```
This method is declared abstract, which means that it should be implemented by a subclass. If you implement the `size` method in the `TreeBasedDictionary` class, you should also implement it in the `AbstractDictionary` class using the `subclassResponsibility` method, as follows:
```ruby
size
    "dictionary size"
    self subclassResponsibility
```
This ensures that any subclass of `AbstractDictionary` that does not implement the `size` method will raise an error.

You asked why you cannot simply implement the `size` method in the `AbstractDictionary` class as follows:
```ruby
size
    "dictionary size"
    ^self size
```
This implementation would create an infinite loop, as calling `size` would simply call `size` again and again. Instead, you should use the `subclassResponsibility` method to signal that the subclass should have implemented this method.

Finally, you should not remove the `size` method from the `AbstractArrayDictionary` class, as this method is still required by the `AbstractArrayDictionary` class and its subclasses. The `subclassResponsibility` method is only used to ensure that subclasses implement the method, not to replace the method in the superclass.

title: Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0
body:
I am learning Pharo online and am not sure if I got the syntax correct for creating class and instance variables. Please correct me if I am wrong :-

Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:

createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone

To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword "new" to create a new instance as under:

CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-

MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:

However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as : 

CreateUser:name:email:phone:

My queries are as below: 1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error? 2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java? 3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?

Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.
stackoverflow_answer:
I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   "and here you probably have something like:"
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   "and so on"

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? "createUsers"? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as "users", and then the responsibility of the class object is "to create users (its instances)".

Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:

initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   phone := userPhone

and a class side method:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   | instance |
   instance := self new.
   instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
   ^ instance

or a shorter version using cascaded messages:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   ^ self new
      initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone;
      yourself

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the "class class". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:

This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.

3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
explanation:
In Pharo, class and instance variables are defined in a similar way, but they are accessed and used differently. The syntax for creating methods in Pharo, whether they are class or instance methods, is as follows: 
createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone
To call this method, you would use the following syntax: * To call the class method, you would use: 
CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone
* To call the instance method, you would first create a new instance of the class using the `new` keyword, and then send the message to the new instance: 
CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone
The error message you received when trying to call the class method is likely due to the fact that you did not include the `Arguments` parameter in your method call. The concept of using class variables/methods in Pharo is similar to Java, but with some key differences. In Pharo, both class and instance variables are defined at the class level, but class variables are shared among all instances of the class, whereas instance variables are unique to each instance. Class methods, on the other hand, are methods that are defined on the class side and can be called without creating an instance of the class. To access instance variables in Pharo, you can define accessor methods (also known as getter and setter methods) to retrieve and modify the values of the instance variables. For example, you could define a `name` accessor method as follows: 
name
    ^ name

name: aName
    name := aName
You can then call these methods to get or set the value of the `name` instance variable: 
| user |
user := CreateUser new.
user name: 'John Doe'.
Transcript show: user name; cr.
This will output: 
John Doe
I hope this helps clarify the syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo! Let me know if you have any other questions. References: * Pharo By Example, 2nd Edition, Stéphane Ducasse, Andrew P. Black, Oscar Nierstrasz, Damien Pollet, and Marcus Denker, Manning Publications, 2016. * Pharo,

title: If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?
body:
Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!

I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?
stackoverflow_answer:
Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.

If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the

  • „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
  • open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
explanation:
To set a breakpoint in Pharo without using "self halt", you can use the "thisContext" pseudo-variable to refer to the current context, and then send the "halt" message to it. For example:

"Add this morph to the world."
thisContext halt.
self openInWorld: self currentWorld

This will cause the debugger to open at the point where the breakpoint is set, regardless of how many times the method is executed.

To set a conditional breakpoint, you can use the "haltIf:" message on the "Object" class, which takes a condition as an argument and only halts execution if the condition is true. For example:

"Add this morph to the world."
self haltIf: [ self sender isKindOf: MorphTest ].
self openInWorld: self currentWorld

This will only halt execution if the message was sent from a method in a "MorphTest" object.

Other ways to get into the debugger in Pharo include:

* Typing "CMD-.", which interrupts the current process and opens the debugger at the point of interruption
* Inserting a "Halt now" expression in code, which causes the debugger to open when the expression is executed
* Running a failing test, which opens the debugger at the point of failure
* Clicking on a line in the stack trace of an error, which opens the debugger at the corresponding method.

title: creation of a class in Pharo Smalltalk?
body:
I have been following the example of making a class in Pharo from the following link: https://ci.inria.fr/pharo-contribution/job/UpdatedPharoByExample/lastSuccessfulBuild/artifact/book-result/PharoObjectModel/PharoObjectModel.html#fig:colorInstanceClassSide

It is the example of making a Dog and a Hyena class. First I created a package called TestC and in the Instance class I have made the following:

For what I know, and correct me if I am wrong, the instance side is where I create the methods that will work when I instantiate an object while the class side it does not need an object to be created to function; just like a static method class in Java.

The first question that I have at this point is why after Accepting the Changes it stills appear the ! symbol in the left part of my classes?

According to the tutorial then I should put the following code:

Dog class
    instanceVariableNames: 'count'

Now I did not where to put it, on the instance side or in the class side, I decided to put it on the instance side the following:

and the last methods that I have are the following, I put them on the class side:

I have tested the classes in the Transcript with the following code:

aDog := Dog new.
Dog count.
bDog := Dog new.
Dog count.

and it works, but I would like to know if I made the right decision to put those methods in the class side (second question), and if its right could anybody give me an example of a method to put in the instance side in this example?

Thanks
stackoverflow_answer:
First Question

The exclamation mark ! in front of the class is an invitation to click on it. In this case it will remind you that classes are supposed to have comments (for the sake of documentation).

Digression

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as

Object subclass: #Dog
  instanceVariableNames: 'name breed birthdate'
  classVariableNames: ''
  package: 'TestC'

For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class

breed: aString
  breed := aString

With this you have a full functional object

dog := Dog new.
dog name: 'Taylor'; breed: 'Great Dane'

However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:

Dog class instaceVariableNames: 'count'

Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Second Question

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question; the fact is that they would have made no sense in the instance side (after all, lucky dogs live exciting lives without ever counting anything.)
explanation:
In Pharo, one can define instance variables and methods by creating a new class and adding instance variables and methods to it. A class can be created in the System Browser by selecting the package where the new class should be added, right-clicking, and selecting 'accept' after filling in the class creation template.

Instance variables in Pharo are private to the instance itself and cannot be accessed directly by other objects. They can be accessed by name in any of the instance methods of the class that defines them and in the methods defined in its subclasses.

Pharo does not have a concept of static or class methods like Java or C++. Instead, it has class-side methods, which are similar to static methods but are actually instance methods of the class's metaclass.

Class instance variables are private to the class itself and are not shared with its subclasses. They can be accessed by any instance method in the class or its subclasses by sending messages to the class. Global variables can be defined by performing 'Do it' on an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier or by executing 'Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil'.

title: How can getter/setter code be generated automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak?
body:
I have a long list of instance variables to create for a class that I want to generate the code for, rather than do it by hand. The list comes from an existing SQL database. My intention is to do it all in a pure object-oriented way with Smalltalk first, and as I learn more, save the data back to the database and work from it directly.

Is there a way of passing the list of names to method that will generate them and add them to the class definition?

In fact is there a way of adding or modifying class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk? I suspect there must and I would like to know a best practices approach.

Update: What I have in mind is more like passing a list of the instance variables to a method that will create them automatically.

It is more like:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')

which will then result in a call to

AddVariables className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')

and

generateAccessors className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')
stackoverflow_answer:
In Squeak, you have Behavior>>addInstVarName: aString, so for instance, you could do something like:

String addInstVarName: 'foo'

Squeak also has refactoring support to generate accessors automatically. You can either use it directly or have a look at AbstractInstanceVariableRefactoring>>createAccessors to get some inspiration on how to implement your own ;-)

Another quite hacky but not so uncommon solution would be to just generate the instance variables, but instead of adding accessors, you overwrite doesNotUnderstand:, which gets called when an undefined selector is sent to your objects. There, you could check if you have an instance variable named according to the message, and return / change it if it is the case. Otherwise you just do super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.

Regarding your comment: Classes are objects, too, so you don't have to do anything special to use them as parameters. On which class you add it is totally up to you and doesn't really matter. So a method to add instance variables could look like this:

addVariablesNamed: aCollection on: aClass

    aCollection do: [:each | aClass addInstVarName: each]

and you could call it like this:

yourObject addVariablesNamed: #('foo' 'bar' 'baz') on: ClassX

You can find examples on how to generate accessor methods in the class CreateAccessorsForVariableRefactoring
explanation:
In Pharo, you can automatically generate getter/setter methods for a class using the convention that a getter method has the same name as the instance variable it gets, and a setter method is named similarly but with a trailing ":". These methods are called accessor methods and are conventionally placed in the accessing protocol.

To add or modify class definitions dynamically, Pharo provides a technique called Dynamic Accessors. This technique traps all messages not understood and checks if an instance variable with the same name as the message sent exists. If it does, the accessor method is compiled dynamically and the message is sent to the object again. This allows for the automatic addition of accessors for instance variables on demand.

Here's an example of how you could define a method to add variables and accessors to a class:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: aClass variablesList: aList
"Add instance variables and corresponding accessors to aClass"

aList do: [ :variableName |
aClass instVarNamed: variableName ifAbsent: [
aClass compile: 'accessing', variableName asString, '^ self ', variableName.
aClass compile: 'set', variableName asString, ': newValue ^ self ', variableName, ' := newValue'.
aClass addInstVarNamed: variableName.
].
].

In this example, the method addVariablesAndAccessors takes in a class name and a list of variable names, and adds instance variables with corresponding accessor methods to the class.

You can also load a library of classes with many methods dynamically by loading a stub for each class in the library that knows where to find the source code of all its methods. The stubs simply trap all messages not understood and dynamically load the missing methods on demand.

In summary, Pharo provides several ways to generate getter/setter methods automatically for a class, including the use of the Dynamic Accessors technique, and dynamic loading of class libraries.

title: Smalltalk - Print something for each 2 seconds
body:
I'm coding in Smalltalk using Pharo. My code:

|i delay|
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
    ] fork.
    i := i + 1.
]

It prints all the "2 seconds are up" at once, and not for each 2 seconds:

2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up

Please, can anyone tell me how to print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk?
stackoverflow_answer:
You forked all 5 processes at once so that all 5 delays ends at the same time. To print every 2 seconds from the background, rather fork a whole loop in a separate process:

|i delay|
[   
    i := 0.
    [i < 5] whileTrue: [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
        i := i + 1]
] fork
explanation:
The issue with your code is that the `fork` method creates a new process, and the code inside the block passed to `fork` is executed concurrently with the rest of your code. This means that the loop that increments the variable `i` and the code that prints the message to the Transcript are executed in parallel, and not sequentially as you might expect. As a result, all the messages are printed at once, after the loop has finished executing.

To fix this issue and print a message every 2 seconds, you can use a timer instead of a fork. Here's an example of how you can do this:

```
| i timer |
i := 0.
timer := Delay forSeconds: 2.
[ i < 5 ] whileTrue: [
    timer wait.
    Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
    Transcript cr.
    i := i + 1.
    timer := Delay forSeconds: 2 ].
```

In this example, a timer is created with a delay of 2 seconds using the `Delay forSeconds:` method. The timer is then started by sending the `wait` message. After the timer goes off, the message is printed to the Transcript and the value of `i` is incremented. The timer is then reset to fire again in 2 seconds. This process is repeated until `i` is no longer less than 5.

It's worth noting that the `wait` message is a non-preemptive wait, which means that it will not cause the process to yield to other processes. This is generally more efficient than using a preemptive wait, such as `sleep`, which can cause the process to yield to other processes and result in additional overhead.

For more information on timers and delays in Pharo, you can refer to the Pharo by Example book, which provides a detailed explanation of these concepts on pages 227-228. You can also find more information on the Pharo documentation website.

title: Why is Smalltalk sometimes called "dynamically typed"?
body:
I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" and not "untyped" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.

There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?
stackoverflow_answer:
"Dynamically typed" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean "dynamically checked". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.

See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
explanation:
Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" because it is a dynamically typed language, meaning that the type of a variable is determined at runtime, rather than at compile time as in statically typed languages. This is in contrast to being "untyped," which would mean that there are no type restrictions at all.

In Smalltalk, there are no type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns. Instead, the type of an object is determined by its class, which is determined at runtime. This means that you cannot compare the type of an object to a specific class or type, because the type is not known until the object is actually created.

Being dynamically typed also does not mean that the language is dynamically type-checked. In Smalltalk, type-checking is not performed at runtime. Instead, the language relies on duck typing, which means that an object is considered to have the correct type if it responds to the messages (methods) that are sent to it.

Here is an example of sending a message to an object in Smalltalk:

```
3 + 4
```

In this example, the message `+` is sent to the object `3`, which is an instance of the class `SmallInteger`. The object `3` has its own method for responding to the `+` message, which is to return the result of adding `4` to itself. This is an example of duck typing, because the language does not check that the object `3` is an instance of a specific class that supports the `+` message. Instead, it simply sends the message and lets the object respond as it sees fit.

In summary, Smalltalk is dynamically typed because the type of an object is determined at runtime, based on its class. It is not dynamically type-checked, because type-checking is not performed at runtime. Instead, Smalltalk relies on duck typing, which means that an object is considered to have the correct type if it responds to the messages that are sent to it.

title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
In Pharo, "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names" are used to define different kinds of variables that are accessible to instances and classes respectively.

Instance Variable Names are variables that are defined for an instance of a class. They are used to store the state of an object and can be accessed by any instance method of the class that defines them and also in the methods defined in its subclasses. For example, if you have a class named MyClass, and you define an instance variable named myInstanceVariable, then each instance of MyClass will have its own copy of myInstanceVariable, and its value can be accessed or modified by any instance method of MyClass or its subclasses.

Class Variable Names, on the other hand, are variables that are shared between all instances of a class and the class itself. They are defined for a class and can be accessed by both class methods and instance methods. Class variables are useful when you want to share some data among all instances of a class and the class itself. For example, if you have a class named Counter, and you define a class variable named totalCount, then the value of totalCount can be accessed or modified by both class methods and instance methods of Counter, and all instances of Counter will share the same value of totalCount.

It is important to note that instance variables are private to the instance itself, whereas class variables are shared among all instances of a class and the class itself. Also, instance variables can only be accessed by instance methods, whereas class variables can be accessed by both class methods and instance methods.

In summary, instance variables and class variables are used to store different kinds of data in Pharo. Instance variables are used to store the state of an object, and they are private to the instance itself. Class variables are used to share data among all instances of a class and the class itself, and they can be accessed by both class methods and instance methods.

title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, and specifically in Pharo, methods return "self" by default because in Pharo, the pseudo-variable "self" represents the receiver of the message, much like the keyword "this" in Java. This design choice encourages a style in which objects tend to have very small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing huge, procedural methods that assume too much responsibility. This principle is known as "Don't do anything that you can push off onto someone else."

When a method does not explicitly return a value, it implicitly returns "self". This is because a method always answers a message with a value, which may be defined by the "^" construct in the method, but if execution reaches the end of the method without executing a "^", the method still answers a value – it answers the object that received the message.

Explicitly writing "^ self" is not a good practice because it communicates that something of interest is being returned to the sender, but in reality, it is just returning the receiver. This can be confusing and may indicate that the method is doing too much.

By default, if not specified differently, a method returns the message receiver. This is a common idiom in Pharo and is used to ensure that the correct object is returned when a class reimplements a superclass method.

This design decision facilitates method chaining, where methods return the receiver, allowing multiple methods to be called in a single statement. This can lead to more readable and concise code.

In summary, methods in Pharo return "self" by default because it encourages small, delegating methods, simplifies method chaining, and ensures that the correct object is returned when reimplementing superclass methods.

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation: In Pharo, the message = tests object equality (i.e., whether two objects represent the same value), whereas == tests object identity (whether two expressions represent the same object). The default implementation of object equality is to test for object identity. However, this default implementation can be overridden in subclasses to provide a more meaningful equality test. For example, the Complex class in the PolyMath package overrides the = message to test for equality of the real and imaginary parts of the complex numbers. If you override the = message, you should also consider overriding the hash message to ensure that instances that are considered to be equal have the same hash value. This is important if instances of your class are used as keys in a Dictionary. The message class can be used to query the class of an object, and the messages isMemberOf: and respondsTo: can be used to test whether an object is an instance of a specific class or responds to a specific message, respectively. Copying objects in Pharo can be done using the copy message, which creates a shallow copy of the object by default. A shallow copy shares references to instance variables with the original object. To create a deep copy, you can use the deepCopy message, which creates a new copy of the object and all its instance variables, recursively.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, control constructs like conditionals and loops are typically expressed by sending messages to objects, with blocks as arguments. This is different from many other programming languages, which often have special syntax for control constructs.

For example, conditionals in Pharo are expressed using the messages `ifTrue:`, `ifFalse:`, and `ifTrue:ifFalse:`. These messages are sent to a boolean object, which determines which block to execute based on its value. The `ifTrue:ifFalse:` method is implemented in the concrete subclasses `True` and `False` as follows:

* `True >> ifTrue: trueAlternativeBlock ifFalse: falseAlternativeBlock`: This method simply executes the `trueAlternativeBlock`.
* `False >> ifTrue: trueAlternativeBlock ifFalse: falseAlternativeBlock`: This method simply executes the `falseAlternativeBlock`.

Here is an example of using the `ifTrue:ifFalse:` method:

```
4 factorial > 20
ifTrue: [ 'bigger' ]
ifFalse: [ 'smaller' ]
>>> 'bigger'
```

In this example, the boolean expression `4 factorial > 20` is evaluated. If it is true, the string `'bigger'` is returned. If it is false, the string `'smaller'` is returned.

This approach allows for a more object-oriented style of programming, where objects are responsible for their own actions and determine how to respond to messages based on their internal state. It also allows for more flexibility and reuse, as methods can be easily shared and reused among objects.

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
In Pharo/Smalltalk, you can read a file with a specific encoding by using the `FileStream class>>on:encoding:` method. This method creates a new file stream that will interpret the content of the file using the provided encoding. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| fileStream |
fileStream := FileStream on: 'test.txt' encoding: 'iso8859-1'.
fileStream binary.
contents := fileStream upToEnd.
fileStream close.
```

In this example, the `FileStream>>on:` method takes as argument a string representing the file name and an encoding symbol. The `FileStream>>binary` method is used to specify that the file stream should be opened in binary mode. Finally, the `FileStream>>upToEnd` method is used to read the content of the file, and the `FileStream>>close` method is used to close the file stream.

You can use the `FileStream class>>encoding` method to get the encoding of a file. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| fileStream |
fileStream := FileStream on: 'test.txt'.
Transcript show: fileStream encoding; cr.
fileStream close.
```

In this example, the `FileStream>>on:` method is used to open the file in the default encoding. The `FileStream>>encoding` method is used to get the encoding of the file, and the `Transcript>>show:` method is used to display it in the Transcript window. Finally, the `FileStream>>close` method is used to close the file stream.

If you are using regular expressions with non-ASCII characters, you can use the `FileStream>>converter:` method to convert the content of the file to the UTF-8 encoding. Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| fileStream |
fileStream := FileStream oldFileNamed: 'path_to_your_file' converter: Latin1TextConverter new.
contents := fileStream contents asUTF8.
fileStream close.
```

In this example, the `FileStream>>oldFileNamed:` method is used to open an existing file for reading, and the `FileStream>>converter:` method is used to specify a converter that will convert the content of the file to the UTF-8 encoding. The `FileStream>>contents` method is used to read the content of the file, and the `FileStream>>close` method is used to close the file stream. The `String>>asUTF8` method is used to convert the content of the string to the UTF-8 encoding.

You can find more information about file streams in the Pharo By Example book, pages 231-234. You can also find more information about regular expressions in the Pharo By Example book, pages 322-346.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
In Pharo, when an object does not understand a message (i.e., a method for the corresponding selector is not found), it invokes the `doesNotUnderstand:` method. This mechanism is part of Pharo's dynamic message passing and late-binding model. By default, when `doesNotUnderstand:` is invoked, a `MessageNotUnderstood` object is created, which can start a debugger in the current execution context. However, you can override this behavior in any subclass of Object to provide alternative ways of handling the error.

This situation may arise due to several reasons:

1. The method for the given selector is not implemented in the object's class or any of its superclasses.
2. The method is implemented, but there is a typo or mistake in the message selector.

To fix this issue, you can:

1. Implement the method in the object's class or one of its superclasses.
2. Verify that the message selector is spelled correctly and matches the method name.
3. Override the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in a subclass to implement custom error handling.

Here's an example of overriding `doesNotUnderstand:` to provide a custom behavior:

```smalltalk
MyMorph >> doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
    "Handle missing methods by logging and returning nil"
    Transcript show: 'Missing method: ', aMessage selector; cr.
    ^ nil
```

In this example, when an object of class `MyMorph` receives a message for an undefined method, it will print a log message and return `nil` instead of raising a `MessageNotUnderstood` error.

- Page 107: The Pharo object model, Message foo is not understood
- Page 312-313: 14.6 Intercepting messages not understood, overriding `doesNotUnderstand:`
- Page 187: Error handling, `doesNotUnderstand:`

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
Pharo, like Squeak, provides an environment for live coding and development with a syntax that is simple, dynamic, and readable even for those unfamiliar with it. The system is built around a live coding environment where code can be modified while it is executing, promoting a fast and efficient workflow. Pharo's tools include:

1. The System Browser: the central development tool used for creating, defining, and organizing classes and methods, as well as navigating through library classes.
2. The Finder: a tool for finding methods, classes, pragmas, and more, with the ability to search by name, source code, pragma, or example.
3. The Monticello Browser: the starting point for loading and saving code in Monticello packages, which is Pharo's version control system.
4. The Playground: a tool for quickly executing Pharo code snippets and storing arbitrary text.
5. The Inspector: a tool for examining and changing live objects in the system.

Interacting with Pharo is primarily done through context-dependent pop-up menus. Clicking on the background of the Pharo window brings up the World Menu, which contains many of the core tools, utilities, and settings.

To launch Pharo from the command line, you can follow the general pattern:

* On Linux: `./pharo shared/Pharo5.0.image`
* On macOS: `./Pharo5.0.app/Contents/MacOS/Pharo shared/Pharo5.0.image`

Replace "shared/Pharo5.0.image" with the path to your desired Pharo image file.

Pharo does not have a built-in system-wide command-line interface like Squeak's `squeak` command. However, you can create your own script to launch Pharo with a specific image and perform tasks using Pharo scripts or the Monticello package manager.

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
In Pharo, you can create a new `Date` or `DateAndTime` object by setting the day, month, and year values using the `day: month: year:` method. However, there is no direct method to create a `Date` or `DateAndTime` object with a specific date value. Instead, you can use the `Date today` or `Time now` methods to get the current date and time, and then manipulate it using the various date and time manipulation methods provided by Pharo.

For example, to create a `Date` object for a specific date, you can use the following code:
```
| date |
date := Date new.
date day: 17.
date month: 12.
date year: 1995.
```
This creates a new `Date` object and sets the day, month, and year values to the specified values.

Similarly, to create a `DateAndTime` object for a specific date and time, you can use the following code:
```
| dateTime |
dateTime := DateAndTime now.
dateTime day: 17.
dateTime month: 12.
dateTime year: 1995.
dateTime hour: 13.
dateTime minute: 45.
dateTime second: 0.
```
This creates a new `DateAndTime` object and sets the day, month, year, hour, minute, and second values to the specified values.

Note that the `Date` and `DateAndTime` classes do not have a `printOn:` method, so you will need to use the `asString` method to display the date and time values in a human-readable format. For example:
```
date asString
>>> '17 December 1995'

dateTime asString
>>> '17 December 1995 at 13:45:00'
```
I hope this helps! Let me know if you have any other questions.
